cataloging_subject.label: An Old English Miscellany (Morris 1872 edited composite)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The edition-level composite delimited by the U-M XML and Richard Morris's 1872 EETS volume: one Bestiary, five Kentish sermons, and thirty-one numbered Miscellanies, with variant versions nested under their count-once works. It is not one medieval work. The XML's thirty-six outer divisions, the Bestiary's thirteen animal chapters, parallel version streams, omitted Latin Appendix I, physical witnesses, print editions, and digital carriers are structural or transmission layers rather than alternate intellectual-item counts.

catalog_summary.title: An Old English Miscellany (Morris 1872 edited composite)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English authors, with Thomas of Hales associated with A Luue Ron and Alfred traditionally credited in the Proverbs title
catalog_summary.editor: Richard Morris (1872); University of Michigan OCR electronic text (2006)
catalog_summary.date: Medieval component works; edited composite published in 1872; U-M encoding released in 2006

titles.value: An Old English Miscellany (Morris 1872 edited composite)
notes: The qualifier makes the edition-level boundary explicit. “Old English” is Morris's historical title wording; the selected texts are cataloged as Middle English.

titles.value: An Old English Miscellany Containing a Bestiary, Kentish Sermons, Proverbs of Alfred, Religious Poems of the Thirteenth Century

agents.name: Anonymous Middle English contributors
notes: A cataloging collective for heterogeneous anonymous works, not a claim of shared authorship.

agents.name: Alfred the Great
notes: Traditional attribution associated with the Proverbs title; not treated as confirmed authorship.

scope.description: The heterogeneous medieval component works as a group; this does not imply one creator or one compilation event

assertion.notes: The title and tradition support an attribution history, not confirmed Alfredian authorship.

scope.description: Selection, transcription, collation, parallel-text layout, and appendices in the 1872 scholarly edition
OCR-derived electronic transcription of selected parts of the Morris edition

date_statements.display: Medieval; the component works were composed and transmitted at different dates, with no single normalized composition date assigned

scope.description: Broad period only; not a claim of simultaneous composition or an authorial collection
Broad origin of the Middle English component traditions, not one proven place of composition

language_statements.label: Middle English

scope.description: The selected intellectual text streams represented in the U-M encoding

notes: Latin and French occur in rubrics, source traditions, comparators, or editorial paratext. Appendix I is an omitted Latin source/comparator branch. These do not make the selected work bilingual.

form_statements.description: The edition-level composite contains thirty-one count-once verse works and six count-once prose works.

genre_statements.label: Edited miscellany
notes: This describes the heterogeneous edition-level cataloging unit rather than forcing one medieval genre across all components.

subject_statements.label: Death, salvation, and the Last Judgment

summaries.value: Richard Morris's 1872 EETS miscellany assembles one verse Bestiary, five Kentish prose sermons, and thirty-one numbered Middle English miscellanies, including devotional, moral, eschatological, proverbial, and descriptive texts. Some numbered works are printed in parallel manuscript versions, and two further versions of The XI Pains of Hell appear as Appendices II and III.

Count-once extent is thirty-seven intellectual items: one Bestiary, five sermons, and thirty-one numbered Miscellanies. The Bestiary's thirteen chapters and all version children have item_count 0. Appendix I (pp. 201-208) is an omitted Latin source/comparator branch. Appendices II-III are zero-count versions of Miscellany XIX.

The selected edition has forty-six work-level text streams: thirty-seven base streams, seven additional paired streams for Miscellany XV and XX-XXV, and two additional Appendix II-III streams for Miscellany XIX. Replacing the Bestiary stream with its thirteen chapter streams gives fifty-eight component-level streams. Neither figure is an alternate intellectual-item count.

extent.description: Thirty-seven count-once intellectual items: one Bestiary, five sermons, and thirty-one numbered Miscellanies. Forty-nine structural content components result when the one Bestiary parent is represented by its thirteen chapters alongside the five sermons and thirty-one Miscellanies. The manifest contains additional zero-count grouping and version records. Raw XML totals of 6,473 L and 414 LG elements are representation metrics that duplicate parallel versions and therefore are not reported as intellectual verse totals.

locators.value: collation source; exact folios not established
collation source; exact appendix locus not established

notes: This branch is evidence for Morris's editorial source work, not a forty-eighth selected text stream or a thirty-eighth intellectual item.

related_works.title: Latin Physiologus and Theobaldus bestiary source tradition
notes: A catalog-level source-tradition entity is used in lineage; no single physical Latin exemplar is asserted.

related_works.title: Maurice de Sully French sermon source tradition
notes: A catalog-level source-tradition entity is used in lineage; incidental French source relations do not make the selected Middle English sermons bilingual.

evidence.citation: Pinned General Corpus XML artifact aha6129.xml.
evidence.locator: TEI header, BODY hierarchy, headings, page milestones, prose paragraphs, verse groups, and lines
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the represented edited selection, headings, hierarchy, language, pagination, and raw XML structure; 362,406 bytes at the reviewed commit.

evidence.locator: 36 outer edition/XML divisions; 37 count-once intellectual items; 46 selected work-level streams; 331 PB pointers with 324 unique refs; 6,473 L and 414 LG elements
evidence.summary: Distinguishes editorial containers, intellectual-item counts, parallel versions, and raw representation metrics. The seven duplicated PB refs are parallel-page overlaps, and raw line/group counts duplicate variant streams.

evidence.citation: Richard Morris, ed., An Old English Miscellany, EETS Original Series 49 (London: N. Trübner, 1872).
evidence.locator: title page; contents; pp. 1-232, especially parallel texts and Appendices I-III
evidence.summary: Immediate edition evidence for titles, the 31 numbered miscellanies, editorial source statements, parallel versions, and Morris's characterization of Appendices II-III as two more metrical versions.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item AHA6129.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Current discovery and access record for the U-M electronic text; identifies OCR production and the selective electronic boundary.

evidence.citation: Direct comparison of Morris pagination, XML PB milestones, and current manuscript manifests.
evidence.locator: Jesus, Cotton, Vernon, and Trinity cached image bundles: source-cache/aha6129/jesus-college-oxford-ms-29-complete-iiif-1800px.zip; british-library-cotton-caligula-a-ix-complete-iiif-1200px.zip; bodleian-vernon-ms-complete-iiif-1600px.zip; trinity-college-cambridge-b14-39-40-complete-iiif-1600px.zip
evidence.summary: Establishes current folio/canvas mappings, including Jesus College MS 29's non-uniform relation between Morris's historical and current foliation, and separates parallel version streams.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue records for Arundel 292, Cotton Caligula A.ix, Add. 27909A, Egerton 613, Harley 3093, Add. 10415, and Arundel 243.
evidence.summary: Current official shelfmark, holding, and manuscript-description evidence for the British Library witnesses and comparator sources.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, Laud Misc. 471.
evidence.summary: Current catalog evidence for the Kentish sermons at ff. 128v-133v and for the distinction between the target text and the repository's selected-image object.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, Douce 302.
evidence.summary: Current catalog evidence for the Appendix II version at ff. 17r-18v; the public manifest is a selected-image object that omits this locus.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, Vernon manuscript.
evidence.summary: Current catalog and digital-surrogate evidence for the Appendix III version at ff. 230r-231r, IIIF canvases 347-349.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, online catalogue and combined IIIF manifest for B.14.39-40.
evidence.summary: Distinguishes the combined physical/digital host B.14.39-40 from target component B.14.39 and locates the Proverbs at B.14.39 ff. 85r-87v, combined-host canvases 173-178.

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright and James Orchard Halliwell, Reliquiae Antiquae, vol. I (1845), pp. 170-188; John M. Kemble, The Dialogue of Salomon and Saturnus (1848), pp. 225-248.
evidence.summary: Supporting evidence for the Trinity-derived Proverbs version reproduced or consulted by Morris; this does not prove that Morris directly transcribed Trinity B.14.39.

evidence.citation: A Catalogue of the Manuscripts Preserved in the Library of the University of Cambridge, vol. II (Cambridge, 1857), pp. 521-522.
evidence.locator: print pp. 521-522 / PDF pp. 533-534; complete ten-line Ten Commandments text on print p. 522
evidence.summary: Supporting catalog evidence for Cambridge University Library Ff.6.15 f. 21r-v; the target is present in the cached catalog, but direct dependence by Morris is not proven.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for aha6129.
evidence.summary: Provides entity separation, scoped transmission relations, access status, rights evidence, and exact local-cache fixity for the edition, witnesses, source traditions, and digital carriers.

open_questions.question: Can complete current-host color surrogates be obtained for witnesses whose public image objects are absent or selected-only?
next_steps: Ask each holding repository first whether an existing complete current-host surrogate can be supplied.

If none exists, request full-color imaging of the entire current physical host, including covers, boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, endleaves, blanks, stubs, and every recto and verso; record the target locus separately.
Accept a partial target-locus order only if complete-host imaging is infeasible, and do not imply quotation, approval, or reuse permission from access alone.

open_questions.question: Did Morris directly consult Cambridge University Library Ff.6.15 or depend on the 1857 catalog transcription for Miscellany XXXI?
next_steps: Inspect Morris's correspondence or fuller editorial records; until then retain only the tentative consulted alternatives for the 1857 catalog and physical witness; do not assert citation or direct transcription.

open_questions.question: How should Morris's Theobaldus source claim for the Bestiary be reconciled with the current British Library description's chiefly Hildebertus attribution?
next_steps: Compare the full Arundel 292 text and current specialist cataloging against Morris's source discussion without collapsing work tradition, manuscript contents, and exemplar identity.

notes: The XML has thirty-six outer edition divisions, but those divisions are not the intellectual-item count.
Miscellany VII has no title beyond “VII.” in the edition; its catalog summary uses the incipit and anti-simony subject without inventing a title.
Miscellany XIX lacks XML N=19, but its sequence and heading are secure.
The U-M XML selectively represents the title page, pp. 1-200, and pp. 209-232; it omits front matter pp. v-xv, Latin Appendix I pp. 201-208, and back matter pp. 233-308.
Latin and French source traditions, rubrics, and paratext are modeled without treating the selected Middle English composite as bilingual.
